Title: Veteran journalist Joshua Purushotman remembered for steady hand, sharp instincts, quick wit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: He was the steady hand in hectic newsrooms and the voice of clarity in public relations — colleagues remember Joshua Purushotman as the consummate journalist who combined sharp instincts with easy wit.
David Christy, who worked alongside Purushotman at the New Straits Times, described him as inquisitive and friendly.
"When he joined us, and even long after that, he would constantly ask me questions about work," said David, who is NST's executive editor for Production.
"With him, it was always 'what other task can I do here?' That's the kind of people you would want by your side in our figurative editorial trenches.
"He joked a lot, too. In the intense times, those days when print was numero uno, and deadlines were unforgiving, his laughter gave us the respite we needed."
Dara Shaipudin, a former colleague of Purushotman's at public relations firm FleishmanHillard said he left an impression on his young group of colleagues he fondly called "young cikus".
"We were just starting in PR, and he really shaped how we think about news, what makes a story, how to pitch it, and why strong media relationships matter.
"Beyond work, he would often share stories from his days in the field, which were both entertaining and insightful.
"To many of us, he was a work father in those early years. Steady, generous with his time, and always willing to guide. We'll miss him."
Sunitha Kanapathy, who worked with Purushotman at Alpha Platform PR said he was a very talented writer.
"We'd give him a few rough notes, and he'd return a media-ready piece in no time. He had a rare gift for cutting through the noise.
"He was our original 'AI tool', only much more instinctive, precise, no fluff, every single time,"
She said his stories and insights from his time in the press helped their colleagues understand the people behind the editorial teams, which helped with pitches.
"Thanks for the lessons and the stories, Puru."
On Thursday, it was reported that Purushotman, died after suffering a heart attack. He was 66.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mercy Malaysia official describes escalations in Lebanon ahead of truce
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Mercy Malaysia head of programme operations Shah Fiesal Hussain recalled how strikes in Lebanon intensified in the days leading up to a 10-point ceasefire plan between the United States and Iran.
He described the situation on April 5 in Beirut as one of the most violent escalations of the conflict.
"I was supposed to leave on April 5, Sunday, but (that day saw)  one of the biggest escalations.
"There were eight bombings in Beirut, in (the suburb of) Dahieh itself.
"I didn't know about the ceasefire. But assessing the situation on the ground, I felt that I needed to leave.
"Things were going to escalate to a different level. So we were looking for flights and we managed to come out at 9pm on April 6," he told the
New Straits Times
.
He said a medicine warehouse behind the Rafik Hariri University Hospital — the largest public hospital in Beirut — was also bombed that day.
Shah Fiesal said  he was surprised to learn of the 10-point proposal and that it was initially agreed upon by the US, as the bombings on April 8 marked a significant escalation in aggression.
He said  attacks would typically take place during the Subuh prayer hour, before sunrise, during midday and during the Maghrib prayer hour.
Al Jazeera reported the Israeli military saying that the attack was its largest coordinated assault on Lebanon since it started a new military operation in the country on March 2, "targeting more than 100 Hizbollah command centres and military sites".
Lebanon's Health Ministry said at least 203 people were killed and 1,000 others wounded in air strikes that targeted areas in Beirut, the Bekaa Valley, Mount Lebanon, Sidon and several villages in southern Lebanon.
Lebanon's Civil Defence put the death toll at over 250.
Shah Fiesal, who had already left Lebanon before the April 8 Israeli attacks, said it was essential for first-line responders to be in heavily affected areas at the height of the attacks as people needed support while on the move.
"We're not paramedics there but we provide other services. When people move there's a lot of uncertainty, a lot of demotivation, mental health issues, anxiety and desperation.
"So any kind of support will motivate them and instil confidence that people are there to assist."
Despite uncertainty on the truce, Mercy's Malaysian team has decided that it will enter Lebanon again in June.
"I hope and pray that things in Syria, Lebanon, Gaza, Yemen will go back to normal. But that's ideal and wishful thinking at the moment".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sustained net inflow of foreign funds signals long-term stability
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sustained capital inflows into Malaysia are expected to reinforce economic stability and provide longer-term support for the ringgit as stronger liquidity feeds into broader growth momentum.
Economists said consistent inflows would underpin business activity, deepen market liquidity and strengthen overall financial conditions.
Malaysia's capital market saw a massive U-turn in March, swinging from a RM2.3 billion outflow in February to a RM6.1 billion net inflow.
In the first quarter of this year, total foreign portfolio inflows reached RM5.8 billion, supported by net inflows into both debt markets of RM4.6 billion and equities of RM1.2 billion.
Universiti Teknologi Mara Business Management Faculty senior lecturer Dr Mohamad Idham Md Razak told Business Times that it may support a firmer ringgit, which helps moderate imported inflation, making goods such as food, fuel or imported products relatively more affordable.
However, several headwinds remain, especially those surrounding global uncertainties.
Idham said geopolitical tensions and fluctuating commodity prices could reverse or slow capital inflows.
Additionally, changes in global interest rates, especially in major economies, may drive investor preferences away from emerging markets like Malaysia.
"Domestically, factors such as policy clarity, fiscal discipline and growth momentum will also influence investor sentiment. As such, while inflows are positive, they remain sensitive to external shocks and confidence levels," he added.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said continued inflows should drive demand for the local currency, driving its rally in the medium to long term.
"We have seen our FBM KLCI reaching 1,896 points in 2018 and perhaps the markets may want to test such levels again prior to the war in Iran.
"Having said that, the foreign investors are the critical element that could push our equity prices and that would mean they need to be convinced on policies and implementation that could lead to earnings accretive going forward," he added.
In its recent report, United Overseas Bank (UOB) said Malaysia is poised to attract more inflows from global investors as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East reshape risk appetite.
Senior economist Julia Goh and economist Loke Siew Teng pointed to three key pull factors: Malaysia's relative stability, resilient macro fundamentals and firmer currency.
"Global investors are likely to reallocate portfolios away from economies with higher exposure to Middle East risks toward relatively lower-risk markets, including Malaysia.
"Malaysia's macroeconomic resilience, firmer currency performance, and relative policy stability compared with regional peers should continue to support investor appeal in the near term," they said.
UOB said in the first quarter of this year, total foreign portfolio inflows reached RM5.8 billion, supported by net inflows into both debt markets of RM4.6 billion and equities of RM1.2 billion.
The latest inflows were largely concentrated in Malaysian Government Securities, which saw net foreign purchases of RM5.1 billion in March.
Idham noted that Malaysian equities are expected to remain resilient but range-bound in the near term, reflecting a balance between supportive domestic fundamentals and external uncertainties.
Defensive sectors such as utilities, banking and consumer staples may perform more steadily, while export-oriented sectors could face some volatility depending on global demand.
"In terms of outlook, the market is likely to trade within a cautious band with mild upside bias, supported by liquidity and earnings stability, but capped by geopolitical risks.
"Overall, the tone is stable but not overly bullish, with investors likely to remain selective," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Supply tightening boosts value for Malaysian palm oil
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian palm oil industry has entered a solid growth phase in March, fuelled by a massive surge in exports that pushes inventories to their lowest level in seven months.
The country's palm oil stocks tumbled 16.1 per cent to 2.27 million tonnes in March from February, marking their lowest level since August, data from the Malaysian Palm Oil Board showed on Friday.
Industry observers said this tightening supply, down for a third consecutive month, demonstrates the global market's strong appetite for Malaysian palm oil.
With demand significantly outstripping production, benchmark futures are poised for a robust rally, further strengthened by rising global energy prices that enhance the value of Malaysia's premier commodity, they added.
Reuters said falling inventories in the world's second-largest producer of the tropical oil could support benchmark Malaysian futures, which have climbed in recent weeks after the Iran conflict drove energy prices higher.
Crude palm oil production increased 7.2 per cent to 1.38 million tonnes, snapping a four-month run of declines. Even so, that was the lowest level of output for the month of March in three years.
Palm oil exports rocketed 40.7 per cent higher to 1.55 million tonness, the most in five months.
A Reuters survey had forecast inventories at 2.18 million tonnes, with output seen at 1.34 million tonnes and exports at 1.56 million tonnes.
"Despite the hefty reduction in March, Malaysian palm oil inventories remain well above typical levels for this time of year," it quoted Anilkumar Bagani, research head at vegetable oil broker Sunvin Group.
"Export performance will need to be repeated in April to support the market, as production so far this month is still seen as elevated, while exports are sharply down in the first 10 days."
A recent rally in soyoil prices has widened palm oil's discount to its rival, potentially drawing Indian buyers back into the market after they stayed on the sidelines last month, said a New Delhi-based dealer with a global trade house.
India's palm oil imports fell nearly 19 per cent in March to a three-month low, dealers said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tanjung Sepat residents at odds over  calls for shutdown of pig farms
Article:
TANJUNG SEPAT:  Residents in Kuala Langat are divided over the sultan of Selangor's call for pig farms in the state to cease operations, with some welcoming the move while others express concern over its wider impact on livelihoods and food supply.
The directive follows longstanding concerns over odour, environmental management and land use involving pig farms in parts of Selangor.
State authorities have said enforcement and restructuring efforts are aimed at ensuring stricter compliance with environmental regulations, including centralised farming systems and proper waste treatment.
Among residents in Tanjung Sepat — a coastal town along the Strait of Malacca — views on the issue vary.
Some said odour from nearby farms was occasional rather than constant.
A resident, who declined to be named, described it as manageable.
"It smells bad once in a while, but mostly we don't even realise there's a farm there," the resident said.
Another resident offered a stronger view, saying: "It smells like a corpse — but that's only on certain days."
Others, however, said the odour was difficult to tolerate when it drifted into residential areas.
"I really can't stand the stench. I am glad they are shutting down the farm," said an elderly resident, who also expressed hope that pig farmers would receive support during the transition.
Meanwhile, a resident, who is also a local food and beverage business owner, offered a more nuanced perspective, highlighting the broader economic and supply chain implications of the closure.
She said she was aware of the directive but did not support it, citing its impact on local food availability.
"I don't think it's right because we use a lot of pork," she said, adding that her business and household consumption could reach dozens of kilogrammes over a few days, increasing significantly during festive periods.
She said her family had relied entirely on local pork for decades, but now had little choice but to switch to imported supply due to shortages.
"We used to use 100 per cent local pork, never imported. But now we are forced to use imported pork because there is no local supply," she said.
The resident, whose family has lived in the area for 62 years and has historical ties to pig farming, said the closure of farms had begun to affect local economic activity.
She said food-related businesses were seeing reduced demand, with customers spending less and buying in smaller quantities. Her own pork usage, she added, had dropped by about half.
Despite concerns over odour, she said living near pig farms had not caused health issues or water contamination.
"We were used to the smell. There is definitely a smell from the manure, but it's like living near a cattle farm," she said.
She also questioned whether recent improvements in coastal water clarity were solely due to the closure of pig farms, noting that other developments, such as the construction of a breakwater, may have played a role.
While acknowledging environmental concerns, she said a complete shutdown may not be the best solution.
"I hope they let the local pig farms continue. For many here, this is a family heritage, a business passed down through generations," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pig farms pose biggest threat to river health in Selangor, say environmentalists
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pig farming is among the biggest threats to rivers in Selangor as rapid industrial growth and weak law enforcement strain water resources in Malaysia's most developed state, environmentalists warned.
Traditional methods that lack proper waste management systems have led to severe environmental and social consequences, particularly its impact on water and public health.
The practice of many traditional farms in discharging untreated pig manure and sewage directly into drainage systems and rivers has led to pollution, threatening clean water supply for millions of residents, not to mention health risks due to foul odour and fly infestation.
Association of Water and Energy Research Malaysia president S. Piarapakaran said rivers and clean water are "the backbone of daily life and industrialisation", with semiconductor and manufacturing industries requiring vast volumes of clean water.
Selangor, the country's most industrialised state and largest contributor to national gross domestic product, faces intense land pressure from housing, agriculture and expanding industry, placing growing stress on its river systems.
A total of 33 rivers nationwide, or 4.9 per cent of those monitored, were classified as polluted last year, Parliament was told.
Experts estimate 81 per cent of Malaysia's drinking water comes from rivers, underlining the high stakes.
"Pig farming collectively contributes the highest biochemical oxygen demand and suspended solids loads to our rivers compared with other sectors," Piarapakaran said.
"This can suffocate aquatic life, collapse ecosystems and trigger toxic algal blooms."
He cited a case in Kampung Selamat, Penang, saying it proved that "even modern pig farming continues to impact river water quality".
He warned the proposed Bukit Tagar pig farm would  pose a serious risk to Sungai Selangor, a key water source.
"An accident from this piggery will trigger an immediate water cut and pollute coastal areas, including fisheries along Kuala Selangor," he said, stressing the potential economic and environmental fallout.
Authorities later scrapped the project, citing risks to water supply.
Sultan of Selangor Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah had decreed he did not consent to pig farming in any district in the state.
Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Minister Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup said none of the 672 rivers monitored nationwide fell into Class V, the most critical category under the Water Quality Index.
"Based on the Department of Environment analysis from 2020 to 2024, pollution loads from pig farming and sewage treatment systems remained consistent and contributed significantly to the country's river pollution," he said.
He added that recurring pollution was driven by population growth, rapid industrial development and illegal waste disposal, warning that "a lack of responsibility" continues to undermine environmental protection.
Piarapakaran said current pig farm effluent treatment was inadequate and must be urgently upgraded.
"We need to go back to the drawing board and revise wastewater discharge standards to improve river water quality and induce a pollution reversal effect. Existing wastewater treatment is inadequate if enforcement is weak.
"We need stricter regulation, real-time monitoring and clear accountability to reverse pollution and restore river health.
"Pig farming must be managed strictly, or we risk repeating past water crises," he warned, referring to Selangor's 2014 water rationing that disrupted households and industries for years.
Sahabat Alam Malaysia president Meenakshi Raman said pollution from livestock farming must be addressed alongside wider sources of contamination.
"Rivers are under multiple stresses from industrial discharge, sewage and agricultural activities. Authorities must take a holistic approach rather than focus on a single source," she said.
She warned that weak compliance allows pollution to persist despite existing regulations.
"Standards mean little without enforcement. Continuous monitoring and firm action against violators are critical if we are serious about protecting water resources," she added.
Former National Water Services Commission chief Charles Santiago said pig farms were not the sole source of pollution.
"There is chicken waste, chemicals, plastics, electronics, sewage and pharmaceuticals," he said.
"We need to stop river pollution. The biggest failure is the lack of enforcement due to a shortage of personnel."
He warned that rising water demand makes protecting rivers increasingly urgent as ecosystems degrade.
"River pollution is very serious and has severely impacted our rivers. It disrupts the ecosystem. You cannot dump pollutants into rivers," he said.
Santiago urged authorities to step up enforcement, increase penalties and stop renewing licences of repeat offenders, stressing that accountability must be non-negotiable.
"If we fail to protect our rivers now, we are effectively gambling with the country's water security and public health," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iranian delegation in Pakistan for talks with US, Vance on his way
Article:
ISLAMABAD: Iran's delegation arrived in Pakistan on Friday for ceasefire negotiations with the United States as Teheran insisted on a truce in Lebanon and release of its blocked assets for the talks to go ahead.
US Vice President JD Vance warned Iran meanwhile not to try to "play" Washington as he headed to Islamabad to represent the United States.
Despite the temporary truce struck between the foes, deep disagreements remain as to the way forward in talks aimed at transforming the fragile ceasefire into a lasting peace deal.
Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said making progress would be hard work.
"A temporary ceasefire has been announced, but now an even more difficult stage lies ahead: the stage of achieving a lasting ceasefire, of resolving complicated issues through negotiations," he said. "This is that stage which, in English, is called the equivalent of 'make or break.'"
Iranian state television said the delegation was led by parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf and reiterated Iran's position that talks would only begin if Washington accepts its preconditions – a Lebanon ceasefire and the unfreezing of Iran's assets.
Israel continued to carry out air strikes in Lebanon on Friday and Lebanon's State Security agency said an attack on the southern city of Nabatiyeh had killed 13 of its personnel.
Lebanon's health ministry meanwhile raised the provisional death toll from massive Israeli strikes across the country on Wednesday to 357 dead and 1,223 wounded.
Lebanon's presidency said a meeting would be held with Israel in Washington next week to discuss a ceasefire in the Israeli war against Iran-backed Hizbollah and the potential start of negotiations between the neighbours.
'OPEN HAND'
Vance, speaking to reporters before taking off for Islamabad, said "if the Iranians are willing to negotiate in good faith, we're certainly willing to extend the open hand."
But "if they're going to try to play us, then they're going to find the negotiating team is not that receptive," he said.
Official sources say the talks in Islamabad will cover several sensitive points, including Iran's nuclear enrichment and the free flow of trade through the crucial oil conduit, the Strait of Hormuz.
Since the ceasefire took effect, President Donald Trump has voiced displeasure at Iran's handling of the strait, which was meant to be reopened, while Teheran has reacted angrily to the continuing Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
Trump posted on his Truth Social network on Friday that Iran has "no cards" in the talks "other than a short-term extortion of the world by using international waterways."
In Islamabad, all routes leading to the Serena Hotel, the expected venue for the talks, were blocked off with heavy security, while a large banner and digital signs along the expressway heralded the "Islamabad Talks."
[[nid:1415083]]
'PURE NOISE'
One 30-year-old resident of Teheran told AFP he was sceptical negotiations would be successful, describing most of what Trump says as "pure noise and nonsense."
"He wants to manipulate the Islamic republic into getting a deal. I think that was his intention, if you can say there is an intention."
A fifth of the world's oil and vast quantities of natural gas and fertiliser pass through the Strait of Hormuz in peacetime, but only a small number of vessels have crossed since the truce was announced earlier this week.
The two-week ceasefire was agreed to allow time for negotiations aimed at ending a conflict that has already killed thousands and plunged the global economy into turmoil.
"I am scared of the war starting again, and at the same time I'm scared of the regime staying," Teheran resident Sheida told AFP, withholding her last name out of concern for her safety.
As Israel continued to carry out air strikes in Lebanon, the Israeli military said Hizbollah had fired around 30 projectiles from Lebanon into its territory on Friday.
Hizbollah said it had targeted Israel's Ashdod naval base with missiles "in response to the enemy's violation of the ceasefire and its repeated attacks on Beirut."
Trump told NBC News on Thursday that Israel was "scaling back" strikes in Lebanon and that Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had assured him its attacks would become more "low-key."
A Western diplomat said on condition of anonymity that there was ongoing "pressure from European states, Gulf states and Egypt on Israel to prevent renewed Israeli airstrikes on Beirut after 'Black Wednesday.'"
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ready to move, but where? Selangor pig farmers seek clarity on relocation
Article:
KUALA LANGAT: Pig farmers, while willing to comply with  the sultan of Selangor's call for the cessation of pig farming in the state, are grappling with a critical uncertainty — where they can relocate.
Farmers told the N
ew Straits Times
that they are prepared to adopt stricter regulations and modernise operations, but the question of relocation, should a state-wide ban be enforced, remains unresolved.
In the meantime, they are actively exploring ways to remain at their current sites through improved waste management and upgraded farming systems.
Third-generation farmer P. Kartigeyan, whose farm in Tanjung Sepat once supplied pigs for export to Singapore, said the situation was deeply unsettling.
"My grandfather started this in the 1960s. My father expanded it, and now I'm running operations," he said.
"Our operations have dropped drastically, by about 75 per cent."
His farm had already scaled back breeding following the African Swine Fever outbreak, even though the farm remained disease-free.
Plans to invest in a closed-house system — an enclosed, climate-controlled facility with strict hygiene controls — and modern waste treatment facilities are on hold pending clearer policy guidance.
"We are ready to improve. We don't want to shut down. This is all I know how to do," Kartigeyan said, highlighting mounting financial pressures as farmers continue servicing loans amid declining revenue.
For decades, many farms have relied on a four-stage system to treat pig waste, involving sediment filtration, biological treatment using effective microorganisms, and repurposing treated sludge as fertiliser.
Kartigeyan said his farm has adhered to these practices, with veterinary authorities conducting inspections biannually.
"Every six months, they take samples. We have not had any issues," he said, adding that monthly treatment costs can reach RM1,000.
However, traditional systems may no longer meet evolving standards.
Among the upgrades under consideration is a mechanical system that separates liquid and solid waste, improving treatment efficiency.
Some farmers, including Kartigeyan, have purchased the equipment — costing up to RM70,000 — but many are holding off on installation until policy clarity is provided.
Another farm in Tanjung Sepat was found operating far below capacity, with many pens empty after the recent swine flu outbreak. Fewer than 15 per cent of enclosures were occupied.
Its manager, Chua, said most livestock had been culled as a precaution.
"Of course, when the farm was running at full capacity, there was a stenchl. It was evident, but tolerable," he said, noting that waste was treated through multiple ponds before discharge.
"We do our best to treat what we can. There is no pollution."
Former Selangor executive councillor Ronnie Liu said farmers are willing to upgrade and work with authorities, stressing that pig farming contributes to national food security.
Discussions between the industry and the state government are ongoing, with proposals focused on modernisation and stricter environmental controls.
The
New Straits Times
has reached out to the state government for comment.
For now, farmers like Kartigeyan and Chua are holding on to hope that a compromise will allow them to continue operating sustainably at their current locations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump's peace board faces cash crunch, stalling Gaza plan, sources say
Article:
JERUSALEM/CAIRO: Donald Trump's Board of Peace has received only a tiny fraction of the US$17 billion pledged for Gaza, preventing the US president from pushing ahead with his plan for the shattered Palestinian enclave's future, sources told Reuters.
Ten days before US-Israeli attacks on Iran plunged the region into war, Trump hosted a conference in Washington that saw Gulf Arab states pledge billions for the governance and reconstruction of Gaza after a two-year pulverisation by Israel.
The plan envisages large-scale rebuilding of the coastal enclave after the disarmament of Palestinian militant group Hamas – whose attacks on Israel triggered the assault on Gaza – and the withdrawal of Israeli troops.
The funding pledges were also meant to pay for the activities of a nascent National Committee for the Administration of Gaza (NCAG), a US-backed group of Palestinian technocrats intended to assume control of Gaza from Hamas.
The Board of Peace denied in a statement on Friday posted on social media after the Reuters story was published that it had funding problems.
"The Board of Peace is a lean, execution-focused organisation that calls capital as needed. There are no funding constraints. To date, all funding requests have been met immediately and in full," it said.
Representatives for NCAG did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
'NO MONEY CURRENTLY AVAILABLE'
One of the sources, a person with direct knowledge of the peace board's operations, said that out of ten countries who pledged funds, only three – the United Arab Emirates, Morocco, and the US itself – had contributed funding.
The source said funding so far was under US$1 billion, but did not give more details. The Iran war "has affected everything," exacerbating previous funding difficulties, the source said.
NCAG could not enter Gaza due to both funding and security issues, the source added. Even after a ceasefire was agreed last October, Israeli attacks have killed at least 700 people in Gaza according to health officials there, while militant attacks have killed four soldiers according to Israel.
The second source, a Palestinian official familiar with the matter, said the board informed Hamas and other Palestinian factions that NCAG is unable to enter Gaza right now due to a lack of funding.
"No money is currently available," the official cited board envoy Nickolay Mladenov as informing Palestinian groups.
[[nid:1415075]]
Hamas has repeatedly said it is ready to hand over governance to NCAG, led by Ali Shaath, a former deputy minister with the Palestinian Authority, which currently exercises limited self-rule in parts of the Israeli-occupied West Bank.
Shaath's committee is meant to assume control of Gaza's ministries and run its police force.
He and his 14 committee members have been cloistered in a hotel in Cairo under supervision by American and Egyptian handlers, said a diplomatic source.
Rehabilitation of Gaza, where four-fifths of buildings were destroyed in two years of Israeli bombardments, has been projected by global institutions to cost around US$70 billion.
The stuttering plan for Gaza's future echoes other ambitious initiatives by Trump, who has sought to project himself as the world's peacemaker but has struggled to end the Ukraine war as he said he would and is seeing this week's truce with Iran come under immediate severe strain.
DISARMAMENT TALKS
Egypt, which has been hosting the disarmament talks, invited Hamas for more meetings on Saturday, according to a source in the militant group.
The ceasefire halted full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone comprising well over half of Gaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip.
Trump's board has been leading negotiations with Hamas and other Palestinian factions on disarmament. Israel says Hamas must lay down arms before it pulls troops out of Gaza, Hamas says it will not comply without guarantees of Israel's withdrawal and a halt to firing in Gaza.
The diplomatic source familiar with the disarmament talks said they remained in deadlock and feared Israel was looking for an excuse to relaunch a full-scale offensive on Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does not lay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began with Hamas' Oct 7, 2023, attacks on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, most of them civilians, according to Gazan health authorities, and has spread famine and displaced most of the territory's population.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Five things to know about the planned Iran-US talks in Islamabad
Article:
ISLAMABAD: Pakistan is set to host talks between Iran and the United States in a bid to turn a fragile two-week ceasefire into a lasting end to a war that has left thousands dead and roiled global energy markets.
Here are five things to know about the Islamabad talks:
The war behind the talks
On February 28, the United States and Israel launched deadly coordinated strikes that killed supreme leader Ali Khamenei and struck Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure.
More than 3,000 people were killed in Iran in five weeks, according to Iranian media and US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA).
Teheran responded by effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz, the Gulf waterway through which about a fifth of global oil and gas passes, sending energy prices soaring and disrupting trade worldwide.
On Apr 8, Washington and Teheran agreed to a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan. The ceasefire is expected to expire Apr 22.
[[nid:1415088]]
Pakistan's unlikely starring role
Pakistan's value as a mediator rests on an unusually broad diplomatic network.
Iran was the first country to recognise Pakistan's statehood following independence in 1947, with the two neighbours sharing a 900-kilometre (560-mile) border and deep historical, cultural and religious ties.
Pakistan is also home to more than 20 million Shia Muslims, the second-largest such population in the world after Iran.
Islamabad has cultivated strong ties with Washington, Riyadh and Beijing.
Pakistani Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar visited Beijing at the end of March for talks with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi, who backed Islamabad's mediation efforts as "in keeping with the common interests of all parties."
Trump himself told AFP that China helped bring Iran to the negotiating table, an account backed by Pakistani officials.
"On ceasefire night, hopes were fading, but China stepped in and convinced Iran to agree to a preliminary ceasefire," a senior Pakistani official familiar with the negotiations told AFP on condition of anonymity.
What's on the table?
The gap between the two sides remains vast.
Washington's reported 15-point proposal centres on Iran's enriched uranium and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.
Teheran has countered with a 10-point plan demanding control over the strait, a toll for vessels crossing the strait, an end to all regional military operations and the lifting of all sanctions.
Lebanon is also a major sticking point.
Israel has continued its strikes in the country targeting Hizbollah – after the ceasefire came into force – with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu rejecting Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif's assertion that the truce included Lebanon.
US Vice President JD Vance appeared to take a softer tone, saying there may have been a "legitimate misunderstanding" from Iran that Lebanon would be included.
Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian warned on X that Israel's strikes on Lebanon rendered the negotiations "meaningless."
Iran has also long refused to concede to Washington's demands on its nuclear programme.
Iranian sources have also told Iranian media that Tehran won't attend the talks unless a ceasefire is in place in Lebanon.
[[nid:1415087]]
Who are the negotiators?
Vance will lead the American team, joined by special envoy Steve Witkoff and presidential son-in-law Jared Kushner.
It marks the most senior US engagement with Iran since Secretary of State John Kerry negotiated the 2015 nuclear deal.
Abbas Araghchi and Iranian parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, along with other security and economic officials, have arrived in Pakistan, Iranian state television and the Pakistani government confirmed.
Reporting their arrival, state broadcaster IRIB reiterated Tehran's position that talks will not begin unless its conditions are met, including a ceasefire in Lebanon.
Islamabad on lockdown
The talks are being held in the Pakistani capital, Islamabad.
The government has kept its cards close to its chest, not confirming the venue, but the Serena Hotel – located next to the foreign ministry in the capital's high-security Red Zone – asked its guests to clear out on Wednesday.
Authorities in the capital announced a two-day public holiday on Thursday and Friday.
The streets of Islamabad are flooded with armed security personnel in military fatigues, traffic diversions are in place and police checkpoints are set up.
The talks are expected to be indirect: the two delegations sitting in separate rooms with Pakistani officials shuttling proposals between them, mirroring the format used in earlier Oman-mediated rounds.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says Iran has 'no cards' beyond Hormuz control
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump said Friday that Iran has "no cards" in upcoming talks with the United States – apart from Teheran's effective stranglehold on the crucial Strait of Hormuz shipping channel.
"The Iranians don't seem to realise they have no cards, other than a short-term extortion of the world by using international waterways," Trump said on his Truth Social network.
"The only reason they are alive today is to negotiate!"
In a separate social media message, the 79-year-old US leader added: "The Iranians are better at handling the fake news media, and 'public relations,' than they are at fighting!"
Control of the narrow Strait of Hormuz, through which one-fifth of the world's crude oil passes, will be at the heart of peace talks between the United States and Iran in Pakistan on Saturday.
Iran and the United States said the crucial channel would reopen after a two-week truce was announced on Tuesday, but Teheran's threats mean very few ships are passing through.
Trump said on Thursday that Iran was doing a "very poor job" of allowing oil through the waterway, adding: "That is not the agreement we have!"
His "no cards" comments about Iran echoed his notorious broadside at Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy in the Oval Office in February when he raged that "you don't have the cards" against Russia.
Separately on Friday, Trump told the New York Post that US warships are being reloaded with weaponry to strike Iran if the talks fail to produce a deal.
"We have a reset going. We're loading up the ships with the best ammunition, the best weapons ever made – even better than what we did previously and we blew them apart," the Post quoted Trump as saying by telephone.
"And if we don't have a deal, we will be using them, and we will be using them very effectively."
In a brief and cryptic social media message earlier, Trump had spoken of the "WORLD'S MOST POWERFUL RESET!!!"
Vice President JD Vance headed to Islamabad on Friday to lead the US delegation in this weekend's talks with Iran, warning Teheran not to "play" Washington.
"We're going to try to have a positive negotiation," Vance told reporters before take-off from Joint Base Andrews outside Washington.
"If the Iranians are willing to negotiate in good faith, we're certainly willing to extend the open hand. If they're going to try to play us, then they're going to find the negotiating team is not that receptive."
In addition to the Strait of Hormuz, other sticking points include US demands that Iran give up its stockpile of highly enriched uranium, and Iran's aim to prevent further US and Israeli attacks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pakistan PM says US, Iran leadership both attending Islamabad talks
Article:
ISLAMABAD: Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said Friday that leaders of both Iran and the United States would attend ceasefire talks in his country – and warned that progress would be hard work.
"In response to my sincere invitation, the leaderships of both countries are coming to Islamabad. There, negotiations will be held for the establishment of peace," Sharif said in an address to the nation.
While US Vice President JD Vance was on his way to Pakistan, there had been a question mark over the Iran delegation after Teheran set conditions for the start of negotiations.
The Islamic republic has said a two-week ceasefire in place must apply to Lebanon, where Israel was still carrying out bombing raids on Iran-backed Hizbollah.
Iran also called for its assets frozen abroad because of US sanctions to be unblocked.
Sharif sounded a more positive note, thanking both sides for agreeing to meet, but said the hard work was still to come.
"A temporary ceasefire has been announced, but now an even more difficult stage lies ahead: the stage of achieving a lasting ceasefire, of resolving complicated issues through negotiations," he said in his televised speech.
"This is that stage which, in English, is called the equivalent of 'make or break'."
He said his government would "make every possible effort to make these talks successful."
On a domestic note, Sharif announced a more than 30 per cent cut in the price of diesel, from 520 to 355 rupees per litre, and a slight cut to petrol prices.
Pakistan had hiked prices just over a week ago in response to spiking global energy prices caused by the Iran war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel won't discuss ceasefire with Hizbollah in Lebanon talks, says envoy
Article:
WASHINGTON: Israel said Friday it will not discuss a ceasefire with Hizbollah when it holds talks with Lebanon's government next week in Washington.
The Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors spoke to set up the talks that will open Tuesday at the US State Department.
Israel "agreed to begin formal peace negotiations" with the Lebanese government, with which it has no diplomatic relations, said the Israeli ambassador, Yechiel Leiter.
"Israel refused to discuss a ceasefire with the Hizbollah terrorist organisation, which continues to attack Israel and is the main obstacle to peace between the two countries," he said in a statement.
Israel launched massive strikes and a ground invasion of Lebanon after attacking Iran on February 28, in response to rocket fire into Israel from Hizbollah, an Iranian-backed Shia Muslim movement.
Without a ceasefire with Hizbollah, the talks would centre on Israel seeking actions from the Lebanese government, which has for years struggled to rein in Hizbollah but has been stepping up its efforts.
Israel has said that a ceasefire between the United States and Iran, which will hold talks starting Saturday in Islamabad, does not cover Lebanon.
Lebanese authorities say the weeks of hostilities have killed more than 1,950 people, with Israeli strikes killing more than 350 people Wednesday alone, the first full day of the US-Iran ceasefire.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Artemis II astronauts hurtle home from moon towards splashdown
Article:
HOUSTON: The four Artemis II astronauts, returning from the world's first crewed moon voyage in over half a century, hurtled back towards Earth on Friday as they prepared their Orion spacecraft for the final phase of their descent and splashdown in the Pacific Ocean off Southern California.
On Friday afternoon, the autonomously piloted Orion crew capsule executed one last eight-second firing of its jet thrusters to fine-tune the flight course, a critical manoeuvre to ensure a safe return.
NASA's celebrated 10-day mission was expected to culminate with the gumdrop-shaped Orion vehicle jettisoning the service module housing its main rocket system, followed by a fiery re-entry through Earth's atmosphere and a six-minute radio blackout before the capsule parachutes into the sea.
If all goes well, US astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Christina Koch, along with Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen, will end up bobbing safely in the ocean aboard their Orion capsule, dubbed Integrity, shortly after 8 p.m. ET (0000 GMT) off the coast of San Diego.
The quartet blasted off from Cape Canaveral on April 1, lofted into an initial Earth orbit by NASA's giant Space Launch System rocket before sailing on around the far side of the moon, venturing deeper into space than any humans before them.
STEPPING STONE TO MARS
They became the first astronauts to fly in the vicinity of the moon since the Apollo programme of the 1960s and '70s. Glover, Koch and Hansen also made history as the first Black astronaut, the first woman and the first non-US citizen, respectively, to take part in a lunar mission.
The voyage, following the uncrewed Artemis I test flight around the moon by the Orion spacecraft in 2022, marked a critical dress rehearsal for a planned attempt later this decade to land astronauts on the lunar surface for the first time since Apollo 17 in late 1972.
The ultimate goal of the Artemis programme is to establish a long-term presence on the moon as a stepping stone to eventual human exploration of Mars.
In a historical parallel to the Cold War era of Apollo, the Artemis II mission has played out against a backdrop of political and social turmoil, including a US military conflict that has proven unpopular at home.
CRITICAL TEST OF HEAT SHIELD
The four Artemis astronauts spent much of the final 24 hours of the mission stowing equipment and configuring the crew cabin for the re-entry and splashdown to come.
The return to Earth will put the Lockheed Martin-built Orion spacecraft through a critical test of its heat shield, which sustained an unexpected level of scorching and stress on re-entry during the 2022 test flight. As a result, NASA engineers altered the descent trajectory for Artemis II in order to reduce heat build-up and lower the risk of the capsule burning up.
Still, with Orion plunging into the atmosphere at some 24,000 miles per hour (38,625 kph), or about 32 times the speed of sound, temperatures outside the capsule are expected to soar to as high as 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit (2,760 degrees Celsius).
As is typical in such return descents, the intensity of heat and air compression will form a red-hot sheath of ionised gas, or plasma, that engulfs the capsule, cutting off radio contact with the crew for several minutes at the start of re-entry.
Moments later, two sets of parachutes will be deployed from the nose of the free-falling capsule, slowing its descent to about 17 mph (27 kph) before Orion gently hits the water.
Just as critical as the performance of the heat shield and parachutes are several other factors, including achieving the spacecraft's precise descent path and re-entry angle through a series of course-correction blasts of its jet guidance thrusters.
The last of three such jet thruster "burns" was conducted on Friday afternoon, roughly five hours before splashdown. A final angle adjustment of the spacecraft was to take place as the vehicle nears the top of the atmosphere.
Once the capsule hits the top of the atmosphere, it takes less than 15 minutes before two sets of parachutes are deployed and the capsule floats into the sea.
NASA says it will take about another hour for recovery teams to secure Orion, assist the astronauts out of the capsule one by one and hoist them into helicopters hovering above.
At the flight's peak, the crew reached a point 252,756 miles from Earth, exceeding the previous record of roughly 248,000 miles set in 1970 by the crew of Apollo 13.
Last week's launch was a major milestone for the Space Launch System rocket, handing its principal contractors, Boeing and Northrop Grumman, long-sought validation that the launch system more than a decade in development was ready to safely fly humans to space.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Designs unveiled for Trump's proposed 250-foot arch in Washington
Article:
WASHINGTON: Designs were unveiled on Friday for a 250-foot (76-metre) arch in Washington sought by US President Donald Trump that, if approved, would reshape the capital's monument-filled skyline.
Trump administration officials formally submitted detailed renderings of the proposed "Triumphal Arch" ahead of a meeting next week to advance the project that Trump has touted for months.
The designs call for an ivory-coloured arch modelled on the Roman Arch of Titus from classical antiquity and resembling the Arc de Triomphe in Paris.
It comes complete with an array of golden fixtures, most notably a towering 60-foot statue on top that resembles the Statue of Liberty with angel wings. She is flanked by two 24-foot eagle statues.
Two phrases from the national Pledge of Allegiance — "ONE NATION UNDER GOD" and "LIBERTY AND JUSTICE FOR ALL" — would be inscribed on each side of the arch. Other design elements include four golden lion statues.
The arch – meant to commemorate the 250th anniversary of the United States this year – would be built at a site currently occupied by an empty traffic circle.
It is located between the Lincoln Memorial and the entrance to Arlington National Cemetery, the military cemetery where around 430,000 individuals have been buried since the Revolutionary War.
Trump's arch plan needs approval from the federal Commission of Fine Arts, which is currently composed of his handpicked allies. Even if the panel greenlights the arch proposal, however, it faces hurdles in the courts. Some Washington-area residents have sued to block it.
Trump told reporters in January that he wants the arch to be "the biggest one of all." At 250 feet, the proposed monument would tower over the approximately 100-foot-tall Lincoln Memorial, would be significantly taller than the Arc de Triomphe, and would be about half the height of the 555-foot-tall, obelisk-shaped Washington Monument in the middle of the National Mall.
It is the tallest new structure proposed by the administration of Trump, a real estate developer, and would be the largest federal monument built in DC since President Franklin D. Roosevelt oversaw construction of the Thomas Jefferson Memorial in 1943.
In other designs also publicised on Friday, the Trump administration is proposing painting white an ornate, grey building next to the White House called the Eisenhower Executive Office Building, where many administration officials work, as well as reimagining Pennsylvania Avenue between the White House and the US Capitol with new roadways and walkways, different trees, and tall American flags.
The president already razed the White House's East Wing to clear a site for a state ballroom, and added his name to the Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, which is set to close this summer for renovations to its theatres and exterior.
Trump also launched a "beautification" campaign around the capital, as federally controlled parks are currently ringed with fencing for ongoing renovations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump warns of fresh strikes if Iran talks fail: NY Post
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump said Friday that US warships are being reloaded with weaponry to strike Iran if talks in Pakistan fail to produce a deal, in an interview with the New York Post.
"We have a reset going. We're loading up the ships with the best ammunition, the best weapons ever made – even better than what we did previously and we blew them apart," the Post quoted Trump as saying.
"And if we don't have a deal, we will be using them, and we will be using them very effectively."
In a brief and cryptic message on his Truth Social network earlier, Trump had spoken of the "WORLD'S MOST POWERFUL RESET!!!"
Vice President JD Vance headed to Islamabad on Friday to lead the US delegation in this weekend's talks with Iran, with a warning to Teheran not to "play" Washington. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah leaders, NGOs urge federal govt to honour 40pct revenue entitlement
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: About 200 people representing 19 non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and political leaders gathered at Padang Merdeka here tonight, urging the Federal Government to honour Sabah's constitutional right to 40 per cent of revenue collected from the state.
The peaceful gathering, held under the Justice4Sabah 40 Per Cent Rights movement, was attended by several leaders, including Tuaran member of parliament Madius Tangau, Ranau member of parliament Jonathan Yasin, Keningau member of parliament Jeffrey Kitingan, Moyog assemblyman Donald Mojuntin and appointed Sabah assemblyman Roger Chin.
Madius, who is honorary president of United Progressive Kinabalu Organisation (Upko), said the gathering marked the beginning of a broader effort by Sabahans, through NGOs and leaders regardless of political affiliation, to ensure the 40 per cent entitlement is fulfilled.
He said Sabahans were hopeful of a positive outcome from ongoing negotiations, following a High Court order issued in October last year on the implementation of the payment, before proceedings were stayed pending an appeal by the federal government.
"Negotiations were supposed to be concluded on April 15, but were postponed after the federal government applied for a stay at the Court of Appeal, which was allowed," he said in his speech.
"We, as elected representatives, have long voiced this issue. The people can only express their voice through the ballot box, but today we begin with a peaceful assembly to demand that our rights be fulfilled."
Former Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim had previously said on Nov 16 that the government was not misleading Sabah over the 40 per cent issue and remained committed to resolving it transparently.
He also said the government would not appeal the Kota Kinabalu High Court decision dated Oct 17 last year, while the Attorney-General's Chambers later confirmed on Nov 11 that negotiations would be held between the federal government and Sabah.
Madius said the rally aimed to ensure that negotiations continue and that all developments are communicated clearly to the people of Sabah.
Jonathan, meanwhile, claimed that Sabah continues to receive an unfair share of federal allocations compared with revenue collected from the state.
"Billions are collected from Sabah, but only a fraction is returned. Our allocation is small compared with the Peninsula," he said.
He added that he was prepared to face political consequences for speaking out on Sabah's rights, particularly the 40 per cent revenue claim.
The gathering, which began at about 6pm, ended peacefully at about 8pm, with about 10 speakers, including NGO representatives, addressing the crowd.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police to intensify operation against drunk drivers, focusing on weekends
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department (TEID) has directed all state contingents to intensify operations against drunk drivers, with a particular focus on weekend nights to reduce the risk of road accidents.
Bukit Aman TEID director Datuk Seri Muhammed Hasbullah Ali said the directive called for more frequent and aggressive nationwide enforcement under Op Mabuk.
"I have ordered that all Op Mabuk operations be strengthened. These operations are not limited to weekend nights but will also be carried out on normal nights, although weekends remain the main focus," he said.
He said this after attending the Appreciation Ceremony for Op Selamat 25 and 26 at the Security Hall, Police Training Centre (Pulapol) here tonight.
Hasbullah said TEID had been placed on standby, with all state contingents instructed to step up enforcement to ensure motorists under the influence of alcohol do not endanger other road users.
"Our aim is to reduce accidents involving drunk drivers. Therefore, enforcement will be enhanced in a more comprehensive and consistent manner," he said.
Earlier, a local man was arrested for
driving against traffic
while under the influence of alcohol at Km0.7 of the Sprint Highway in Petaling Jaya early yesterday.
Petaling Jaya district police chief Assistant Commissioner Shamsudin Mamat said the man was detained at 2.10am after being spotted driving dangerously, with subsequent tests confirming he was intoxicated.
On April 3, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said suggested that laws relating to road traffic offences be reviewed to ensure
harsher penalties
and stronger deterrent effect.
She said this in response to a fatal crash in Klang, Selangor involving a driver under the influence of drugs and alcohol, which claimed the life of motorcyclist Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, on March 29.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DeepSeek's V4 model to test China's AI ambitions
Article:
FOR weeks now, the global tech industry has been waiting for a major artificial intelligence (AI) launch from DeepSeek, seen as a benchmark for China's progress in the fast-moving field.
More than a year has passed since the startup put Chinese AI on the map in early 2025 with a low-cost chatbot that performed at a similar level to US rivals.
But despite reports and rumours about its imminent release, DeepSeek's next-generation "V4" model is nowhere in sight.
Speculation is also swirling over the geopolitical implications of which computer chips were chosen to train and power the new system: world-leading US designs or made-in-China alternatives that the country is racing to develop.
"It's important to know because at one level, it is a signal of China's AI self-sufficiency trajectory," said Wei Sun, principal AI analyst at Counterpoint Research.
Tech news outlet
The Information
reported last week that V4 could be run on the latest chips made by China's Huawei.
Such a shift would mark a milestone for China in its bid to beat United States restrictions on the export of top-of-the-range AI chips from Californian titan Nvidia to the country.
The report cited five people with direct knowledge of large orders for Huawei chips, made in preparation for the DeepSeek launch by tech giants including Alibaba, ByteDance and Tencent.
AFP contacted DeepSeek, Huawei, Alibaba, ByteDance and Tencent but none were able to comment.
DeepSeek started life in 2023 as a side project of a hedge fund that had access to a cache of powerful Nvidia processors.
It shot to attention in January last year with its R1 deep-reasoning chatbot, which sent US tech shares tumbling with President Donald Trump calling it a "wake-up call" for American firms.
R1 was based on DeepSeek's last major AI model, V3, which was released in December 2024.
The company's affordable, customisable AI tools have been widely adopted in China, and are also popular in emerging markets such as Southeast Asia and the Middle East.
Stephen Wu, founder of the Carthage Capital fund, said V4 — said to be multimodal, meaning it can generate text, pictures and video — could again shock US tech valuations.
"I expect the upcoming DeepSeek V4 release will not just be a software update; it will be a highly capable, open-source model that handles massive context windows at a fraction of the cost," he predicted.
But DeepSeek's reputation as a company at the frontier of AI technology is also at stake.
Its models previously relied on Nvidia chips, so a move to collaborate with domestic chipmakers would require "substantial re-engineering", said Wei.
"That transition can slow development cycles and introduce performance trade-offs, especially for V4, a model expected to be state-of-the-art."
The US cites national security concerns as the reason for its export ban on Nvidia's most powerful AI processors to China.
"The ongoing wait for DeepSeek V4 points to friction in scaling advanced models without unrestricted access to top-tier Nvidia hardware," Wu added.
But some reports allege that DeepSeek skirted the ban to train V4 using thousands of Nvidia's top-end Blackwell chips, dismantled in third countries and smuggled to China.
Training AI models requires huge amounts of computing power — much more than for processing generative AI queries, which is known as inference.
AFP has contacted DeepSeek for comment. Nvidia did not respond to a comment request but told
The Information
it had not seen evidence of this and "such smuggling seems farfetched".
Another Chinese AI startup, Zhipu, in January unveiled an image generator that it said
had been entirely trained on Huawei chips.
And Alibaba said this week it would open a new data centre for AI training and inference in southern China, powered by 10,000 of its own chips and operated by China Telecom.
As for DeepSeek, "if they have successfully trained V4 entirely on Huawei silicon, it signals a material shift in the geopolitical tech landscape", said Wu.
The writers are from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police: Autistic girl likely left home before drowning in retention pond
Article:
JITRA: An autistic girl who was reported missing from the People's Housing Project (PPR) at Taman Nagasari, Kepala Batas, earlier today before being found drowned in a retention pond in the afternoon is believed to have left her home on her own through the gate.
Kubang Pasu district police chief, Superintendent Mohd Radzi Abdul Rahim, said initial investigations found that the gate was believed to have been opened by the victim's nine-year-old autistic brother.
He said the complainant, who is the victim's father, was informed by his eldest child, aged 20, that Mya Khadeeja Suraidi, 6, was missing and believed to have exited the house through the gate.
"Following the report, a team of officers and personnel was deployed to the housing area to assist in the search. Focus areas included the retention pond, large drains and other high-risk locations around Taman Nagasari.
"At 4.10pm, police were informed of the discovery of the child, who is believed to have drowned in a retention pond next to the surau at Taman Nagasari PPR," he said when contacted today.
Mohd Radzi said initial forensic checks by the Kedah police contingent's Criminal Investigation Department found no signs of criminal elements or injuries caused by other individuals on the victim's body.
He said the body had been sent to hospital for further action, and the case is being investigated under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001.
Earlier reports stated that the search for Mya Khadeeja, who went missing this morning, ended in tragedy when she was found drowned in the retention pond in the afternoon.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melania's Epstein comments mark rare public foray for 'unknowable' first lady
Article:
MELANIA Trump, an enigmatic former model from Slovenia, has occupied the role of US first lady on her own terms.
She has often stayed silent on political issues or signalled tacit support for her husband, President Donald Trump.
Thursday's press conference decrying the idea that she had any relationship with sex offender Jeffrey Epstein marked one of the few times she has addressed a group of journalists directly.
Intensely private for such a high-profile figure, Melania Trump made only eight speeches in her husband's first year in office in 2017.
By comparison, Michelle Obama made 74 in 2009, the first year of her husband Barack Obama's presidency.
"She is very unknowable," Kate Andersen Brower, author of the book "First Women: The Grace and Power of America's Modern First Ladies," told Britain's The Times in 2024.
But the first lady has occasionally made waves publicly, such as when she backed her husband's false assertions of victory after the 2020 presidential election.
"Every legal – not illegal – vote should be counted," she wrote, echoing language used by him to claim that he had been the victim of electoral fraud.
In 2018, she caused a media frenzy by wearing a jacket emblazoned with the words: "I REALLY DON'T CARE, DO YOU?" for a flight to Texas to visit migrant children separated from their parents.
Years later, she said the jacket was a response to critics in the media.
This year, she released a movie documenting her life in the 20 days leading up to her husband's second inauguration.
"Melania" was touted as giving viewers rare behind-the-scenes access to her, although critics said it failed to offer much in the way of new insight.
Melania, 55, is Donald Trump's third wife and the first US first lady to be a naturalised citizen.
She started modelling at 16, eventually travelling to Paris and Milan for her career before moving to New York in 1996.
She met her future husband at a New York party two years later and married him in January 2005.
Donald Trump has compared her to the famously glamorous Jackie Kennedy, later Jackie Onassis. In 2019 he told the Fox News "Fox and Friends" TV show:
"We have our own Jackie O today. It's called Melania. We'll call it Melania T."
"People love her. She gets no credit from the media, but she gets credit from the people," Trump said.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Refined fuel prices retreat in Asia, but still show supply stress
Article:
PRICES for refined fuels in Asia fell sharply in line with similar declines for crude oil in the wake of the tentative ceasefire between the United States and Iran, but remain at levels that point to a supply crunch.
Prices for gasoil, jet fuel and petrol in the Asian trading hub of Singapore all saw double-digit drops on Wednesday, amid market relief that the deal may lead to a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.
However, the ceasefire and commitment to peace talks made in separate announcements by the US and Iran already looks to be on the ropes.
Teheran suggested it would be "unreasonable" to proceed with talks to forge a permanent deal with the US as long as Israel continues to attack the Iran-aligned Hizbollah group in Lebanon.
There were reports of some vessels going through the Strait of Hormuz in the wake of the deal, but it remains to be seen whether more ship owners will risk transiting the narrow waterway through which as much as 20 per cent of global crude oil, refined products and liquefied natural gas moved prior to the US and Israeli attack on Iran on Feb 28.
Even if tanker movements do pick up, the market for physical refined products in Asia still looks stressed and will likely remain so for an extended period.
Global benchmark Brent crude futures ended at US$94.75 a barrel on Wednesday, down 13.3 per cent from the previous close.
Brent ended at US$72.48 on Feb 27, meaning it has gained 30.7 per cent from the start of the Iran conflict.
While the increase in Brent looks strong, it pales in comparison to the surge in prices for refined products in Asia.
The hardest hit part of the barrel has been jet fuel, given it is more challenging to store.
Singapore jet fuel ended at US$193.53 a barrel on Wednesday, down 14.2 per cent from its previous close and 20 per cent below the record high of US$242.06 reached on March 30.
However, it is still more than double the US$93.45 it closed at on Feb 27, the day before the US and Israel launched their aerial campaign against Iran.
Gasoil, the building block for diesel, ended at US$145.02 a barrel on Wednesday, dropping 17.1 per cent from the previous close, but it is still 59 per cent higher than the close on Feb 27.
Petrol finished at US$120.80 a barrel on Wednesday, down 13 per cent from the previous close. The light vehicle fuel is up 52 per cent from the close on Feb 27.
The premiums being commanded by refined fuels over crude futures reflect that many Asian refiners are struggling to secure sufficient oil in order to maintain operating rates.
Seaborne crude imports into Asia are estimated at 19.22 million barrels per day (mbpd) for April, according to data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
This is down from the three-month moving average of 25 mbpd for the first quarter.
It's also worth noting that April saw the last of the arrivals of vessels that exited the Strait of Hormuz prior to its effective closure after the conflict started.
This means that seaborne arrivals into the top-importing region are likely to be lower next month, even if more tankers start to move through the strait.
The drop in crude arrivals is already showing up in shipments of refined products, with Kpler data estimating this month's exports from Asian refiners at 6.61 mbpd, down from the 7.32 mbpd last month.
April and March were the two softest months for refined fuel exports in Asia since April 2017, according to Kpler data, and are well down from the 11.1 mbpd recorded in February.
It's the loss of around 5.0 mbpd of refined product exports in Asia that is keeping fuel prices elevated, and even if crude does start to flow out of the Middle East at pre-conflict levels, the supply chain will take months to catch up.
The risk is that the situation worsens in the near term, especially if the ceasefire fails and the Strait of Hormuz remains off-limits to most vessels.
The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KJ will accept Umno decision on rejoin bid, says it is too early for politics
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: Former Umno Youth chief Khairy Jamaluddin says he will accept whatever decision is made by the party's Supreme Council regarding his application to rejoin Umno.
He said he understood that his application would be tabled and decided by the Supreme Council on April 17.
"On the matter of my status (application to rejoin Umno), I have met the president (Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi) before Hari Raya, and I was informed that on April 17, the application will be tabled at the Supreme Council and decided on the same day.
"Whatever decision the party makes, I will accept it," he told reporters when met at the Bertam state assemblyman Datuk Seri Reezal Merican Naina Merican's open house here tonight.
Earlier, several former Umno leaders, including Khairy and Datuk Seri Hishammuddin Hussein, were reported to have submitted applications to rejoin the party last month.
Hishammuddin; former Umno information chief Shahril Sufian Hamdan; Jempol Umno division chief Datuk Seri Mohd Salim Sharif; and former Tebrau Umno division chief Datuk Maulizan Bujang were previously suspended for six years.
Former Selangor Umno liaison chief Tan Sri Noh Omar and Khairy were expelled from the party following disciplinary breaches during the 15th General Election.
The suspensions and expulsions were decided by the Umno Supreme Council on Jan 27, 2023.
Meanwhile, when asked about speculation that he might contest the Kepala Batas parliamentary seat, Khairy said it was too early to discuss such matters as his application to rejoin Umno was still pending.
"I am only in the process of rejoining Umno, so it is too early to talk about which seat I will contest. My previous membership was with the Sungai Buloh division.
"Secondly, decisions on candidacy under Barisan Nasional are made by the chairman, Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi. So we should not pre-empt such matters.
"But most importantly for me, when Allahyarham Tun Abdullah (Ahmad Badawi) was still around, he reminded us — the two of us were his protégés; I am his son-in-law, and Reezal is his political protégé — that Kepala Batas should be looked after by Reezal. I would not go against the wishes and respect for my late mentor," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israeli strike in south Lebanon kills 10 security personnel
Article:
BEIRUT: An Israeli strike, one of multiple on the southern Lebanese city of Nabatiyeh on Friday, killed at least 10 State Security personnel, a source from the government agency told AFP, as Lebanon prepared to start ceasefire talks with Israel.
Naim Qassem, head of Iran-backed party and armed group Hizbollah, called on the Lebanese state to stop giving "free concessions" to Israel, with the two governments due to begin negotiations next week to end the war that has left nearly 1,900 people in Lebanon dead.
The government banned Hizbollah's military activities at the beginning of the latest war with Israel in March and is moving towards bilateral negotiations with Israel despite the opposition of Hizbollah, which is represented in the cabinet and parliament.
The state-run National News Agency (NNA) said "enemy warplanes launched a series of heavy strikes" on Nabatiyeh, including one in the vicinity of the government complex hitting the State Security office.
An AFP photographer saw extensive damage at the site, where a fire was still raging.
- Diplomatic scramble -
As the government prepared for talks with Israel in Washington, outside the auspices of the US-Iran talks in Islamabad, Qassem called on officials "to stop offering free concessions" and described Israel's military campaign as a failure.
"The Israeli enemy has failed on the battlefield... It has been unable to carry out the ground invasion it repeatedly announced," he said, adding that "the resistance will continue until the last breath."
More than 300 people, mostly civilians according to a Lebanese military source, were killed in a wave of simultaneous Israeli strikes on Lebanon on Wednesday despite the announcement hours earlier of a truce between the United States and Iran, with Israel and the US saying it did not apply to Lebanon.
Iran has insisted on including Lebanon in its ceasefire negotiations with the US.
- Quiet in Beirut -
On Thursday afternoon, the Israeli military issued a warning of incoming strikes for large, densely populated areas of southern Beirut, but had not carried out the threat as of Friday, with a Western diplomat telling AFP Friday that European and Arab states are pressuring Israel to stop targeting Beirut.
The Western diplomat, who asked to remain anonymous in order to discuss sensitive matters, said on Friday "There is ongoing diplomatic pressure from European states, Gulf states and Egypt on Israel to prevent renewed Israeli airstrikes on Beirut after 'Black Wednesday'."
Thursday's Israeli warning included areas home to major hospitals and the road to the country's only international airport.
Public Works and Transport Minister Fayez Rasamni said, in a statement carried by the state-run National News Agency (NNA) on Thursday, that he had "received assurances" from foreign diplomats that the airport and the road leading to it would be spared.
Meanwhile, Mohammad Zaatari, director of the country's largest public medical facility, Rafic Hariri Hospital, told AFP: "We have received assurances, including from the International Committee of the Red Cross that the hospital would not be targeted."
The World Health Organisation on Thursday called on Israel to cancel its evacuation warning for the Jnah district of Beirut because around 450 patients were in the Rafic Hariri and Al-Zahraa hospitals in the district, including 40 in intensive care.
The Israeli military said on Friday it had "dismantled" more than 4,300 Hizbollah sites in Lebanon and killed "more than 1,400" Hizbollah fighters.
Hizbollah, for its part, claimed several rocket launches on northern Israel, as well as attacks on Israeli troops advancing in the border area.
Hizbollah also said it targeted a naval base in the southern Israeli city of Ashdod on Friday, far from the border, with missiles. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-EC chairman Mohd Hashim Abdullah laid to rest
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Former Election Commission (EC) chairman Tan Sri Mohd Hashim Abdullah was laid to rest at the Precinct 20 Muslim Cemetery here at 10.20pm tonight.
The hearse carrying his remains arrived at the cemetery at 9.30pm.
About 150 people, including family members, friends and EC staff, attended the funeral.
Earlier, funeral prayers were performed at Surau Al-Mizan in Precinct 8, led by his eldest son, Arif Izwan, with more than 100 people in attendance.
Among those present to pay their last respects were Communications Ministry secretary-general Datuk Abdul Halim Hamzah, Putrajaya MP Datuk Dr Radzi Jidin, former Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Economy) Datuk Seri Mustapa Mohamed, former senior deputy secretary-general (Prime Minister's Department) Tan Sri Othman Mahmood and former Treasury secretary-general Tan Sri Mohd Irwan Serigar Abdullah.
Hashim, 71, born in Kota Baru, Kelantan,
died at the National Heart Institute
(IJN) at 2pm today due to health complications.
Bernama reported that he had suffered a heart attack last Tuesday before being rushed to Putrajaya Hospital.
He was referred to IJN at 10am today but suffered another heart attack en route and was pronounced dead upon arrival.
Hashim was appointed EC chairman on Jan 24, 2016, and served until July 1, 2018.
He had previously held several senior government positions, including senior private secretary to the youth and sports minister, senior private secretary to the rural development minister, and deputy secretary-general (Entrepreneurs) at the Entrepreneur and Cooperative Development Ministry.
He also served as secretary-general of the Agriculture and Agro-Based Industry Ministry from Nov 11, 2010 to Aug 31, 2014, upon reaching mandatory retirement age.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: How to give people access to justice
Article:
MALAYSIA Mediation Centre's (MMC) initiative to offer free legal assistance to the B40 and M40 groups could mark a turning point in making justice accessible, affordable and efficient, especially for the middle majority often excluded from legal recourse.
The B40 and M40 are the most likely to fall through the cracks — not poor enough for full legal aid, not wealthy enough for sustained litigation.
The MMC initiative offers speed, lower costs and less adversarial outcomes — preserving relationships in family, community and business disputes. If scaled, it could also ease the burden on courts, allowing judges to focus on more complex cases.
Perhaps the most significant impact lies in changing how Malaysians perceive justice itself. For many, justice is synonymous with courtroom battles and definitive victories.
If embraced, this could foster a more constructive dispute-resolution culture, reducing hostility and encouraging dialogue.
Here is why MMC's execution of free legal assistance is important. In Kekatong Sdn Bhd v Danaharta Urus Sdn Bhd, the Court of Appeal declared thus: Access to justice is an integral part of Article 8(1) of the Federal Constitution. Malaysian lawyers, too, have expressed similar views.
To them, access to justice is a fundamental human right, rooted in civilised values, religion and the Federal Constitution. It may be odd to talk of civilised values in a world where these very values are being threatened, but it doesn't mean they are not there.
As one lawyer pointed out, all religions lay great emphasis on doing justice. The Quran, he writes in his article published by the Malaysian Bar, teaches the faithful to stand out firmly for justice even against oneself, parents, kin or whether against the rich or poor.
Speaking in the context of Malaysia, every Malaysian has the right to fair adjudication before an independent judicial tribunal in any dispute with public authorities or any person.
The Supreme Court in the United Kingdom, too, speaks of people's constitutional right of access to justice as inherent in the rule of law.
In Unison R v Lord Chancellor, the Supreme Court said: "Without such access, laws are liable to become a dead letter, the work done by Parliament (primarily making laws) may be rendered nugatory, and the democratic elections of members of parliament may become a meaningless charade."
In other words, people must have unimpeded access to courts and tribunals.
For far too long, the B40 and M40 have been denied access to justice mainly because of the costly legal process. MMC's announcement to provide free legal assistance to the two groups is certainly a welcome move.
But the real test, however, is not in its announcement but in its execution.
